---
title: "Tutorial for the quality control of climate data with agrometflow"
author: "agrometflow"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
    self-contained: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 5
execute:
  kernel: python3
  warning: false
jupyter: agrometflow
---

## Introduction

This page illustrates how to use the **agrometflow** plotting functions for visual
quality control of climate data. These functions are inspired by the R package
[dataresqc](https://github.com/c3s-data-rescue-service/dataresqc) by Yuri Brugnara & Stefan Hunziker.

The functions covered here are:

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `plot_daily` | Daily time series with missing-data indicators |
| `plot_decimals` | Distribution of decimal digits (reporting resolution) |
| `plot_weekly_cycle` | Wet-day fraction by day of week (data-rescue bias test) |
| `plot_subdaily` | Sub-daily data organized by month |

All functions display inline in Jupyter/Quarto by default (`show=True`) and
can optionally save to PDF (`outfile=` or `outpath=`).

---

## Setup

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import (
    plot_decimals,
    plot_daily,
    plot_subdaily,
    plot_weekly_cycle
)
```

---

## Load sample data

We use a daily climate series downloaded from [NASA POWER](https://power.larc.nasa.gov/)
for a location in West Africa (lat 12.5°N, lon -1.2°E), covering 1981–2020.

```{python}
csv_path = Path("data/climate/power/power_19810101_20201231.csv")

clim_df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
clim_df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(clim_df["Date"], format="%Y%m%d", errors="coerce")

print(f"Records : {len(clim_df)}")
print(f"Period  : {clim_df['Date'].min().date()} → {clim_df['Date'].max().date()}")
print(f"Columns : {list(clim_df.columns)}")
clim_df.head()
```

---

## Daily time series {#sec-daily}

The function `plot_daily` plots a daily time series with one panel per year
(or group of years). Missing dates are marked with red crosses at the bottom
of each panel.

### One year per panel

```{python}
plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=2010,
    endyear=2012,
    years_per_panel=1,
    show_missing=True,
    show=True,
    color="steelblue",
    linewidth=0.8
)
```

### Multiple years per panel

You can group several years in a single panel using `years_per_panel`:

```{python}
plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    years_per_panel=5,
    show_missing=False,
    show=True,
    color="darkorange",
    linewidth=0.6,
    alpha=0.8
)
```

---

## Distribution of decimals {#sec-decimals}

The function `plot_decimals` examines the **reporting resolution** of a series
by counting how often each decimal digit (0–9) appears in the data.

A uniform distribution across all decimals indicates 0.1-unit precision.
Systematic over- or under-representation of certain decimals is a sign of
rounding, conversion artefacts, or digitization biases.

```{python}
plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    show=True
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
In this NASA POWER example, the decimals are distributed fairly uniformly —
expected for a gridded reanalysis product. For digitized station data, you
would typically see spikes at `.0` or `.5` (0.5°C or 1°C resolution).
:::

---

## Weekly cycle in precipitation {#sec-weekly}

The function `plot_weekly_cycle` tests whether wet-day frequency varies
systematically by day of week using a **binomial test** (Hunziker et al., 2017).

This is useful for detecting data-rescue biases: if a human observer tended
to skip or accumulate observations on certain days of the week, a systematic
pattern would appear.

Bars shown in **red** indicate a statistically significant deviation from the
overall wet-day probability (dashed line).

```{python}
results = plot_weekly_cycle(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "PR"]].rename(columns={"PR": "rr"}),
    date_col="Date",
    precip_col="rr",
    station_id="POWER_station",
    threshold=1.0,
    confidence_level=0.95,
    show=True
)

print(f"\nOverall wet-day probability φ = {results['phi']:.3f}")
print("\nSignificant deviations by day of week:")
found = False
for wd, sig, frac in zip(results["weekdays"], results["significant"], results["fractions"]):
    if sig:
        direction = "↑" if frac > results["phi"] else "↓"
        print(f"  {wd}: {frac:.3f} {direction}")
        found = True
if not found:
    print("  None — no significant weekly cycle detected.")
```

---

## Sub-daily data {#sec-subdaily}

The function `plot_subdaily` plots sub-daily observations organized by month,
with one panel per month (4 × 3 grid). This is useful for inspecting
digitized historical sub-daily data for transcription errors, time-of-day
biases, or systematic shifts.

Here we illustrate with synthetic hourly temperature data following a realistic
annual + diurnal cycle:

```{python}
dates = pd.date_range("2020-01-01", "2020-12-31 23:00", freq="h")
np.random.seed(42)
synthetic_temp = (
    20
    + 10 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / (24 * 365) - np.pi / 2)
    +  6 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / 24 - np.pi / 2)
    +  np.random.randn(len(dates)) * 1.5
)

subdaily_df = pd.DataFrame({"datetime": dates, "value": synthetic_temp})

plot_subdaily(
    data=subdaily_df,
    datetime_col="datetime",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Temperature (synthetic)",
    units="°C",
    year=2020,
    fixed_ylim=True,
    show=True,
    marker=".",
    markersize=1,
    linestyle="",
    alpha=0.5,
    color="royalblue"
)
```

---

## Save plots to PDF

All functions accept an optional `outfile` (or `outpath` for `plot_weekly_cycle`)
parameter to save the plot to a PDF in addition to displaying it.

```{python}
# Example: save decimals plot to PDF and display inline
out_dir = Path("data/qc")
out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=2000,
    endyear=2020,
    outfile=out_dir / "decimals_TMAX.pdf",
    show=True
)
```

---

## GHCN-Daily station data {#sec-ghcnd}

The `GHCNDDownloader` fetches station observations from the
[NOAA GHCN-Daily](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/land-based-station/global-historical-climatology-network-daily)
database — the largest archive of daily climate records (~120 000 stations worldwide).

### Search available stations

```{python}
from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader

dl = GHCNDDownloader(verbose=True)

# Search stations inside a bounding box (West Africa example)
bbox = (-5.0, 10.0, 5.0, 16.0)   # (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat)
stations = dl.search_stations(bbox=bbox)
print(f"Stations found: {len(stations)}")
stations.head(10)
```

### Check variable inventory

Before downloading, inspect which variables and years are available:

```{python}
inventory = dl.get_inventory()

# Filter to our bounding box stations and core temperature variables
sids = stations["station_id"].tolist()
inv_sub = inventory[
    inventory["station_id"].isin(sids)
    & inventory["variable"].isin(["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
].sort_values(["station_id", "variable"])

print(f"Inventory rows: {len(inv_sub)}")
inv_sub.head(15)
```

### Download by station ID

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Pick one station as an example
station_id = stations.iloc[0]["station_id"]
print(f"Downloading station: {station_id}")

dl.download(
    station_ids=[station_id],
    start_date="2000-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
)

df_station = dl.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Records: {len(df_station)}")
df_station.head()
```

### Download all stations in a bounding box

```{python}
#| eval: false
dl_bbox = GHCNDDownloader()

dl_bbox.download(
    bbox=bbox,
    start_date="2010-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
    min_years=5,          # skip stations with < 5 years of overlap
    max_workers=8,
)

df_bbox = dl_bbox.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Stations: {df_bbox['station'].nunique()}")
print(f"Records : {len(df_bbox)}")
df_bbox.head()
```

### Plot downloaded GHCN-Daily data

Once downloaded, the output CSV can be read back and plotted with the same
functions as POWER data:

```{python}
#| eval: false
from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import plot_daily, plot_decimals

dl = GHCNDDownloader()
df = dl.extract(source="data/ghcnd/ghcnd_20100101_20201231.csv",
                variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])

# Plot daily TMAX for first station
sid = df["station"].iloc[0]
df_s = df[df["station"] == sid][["date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"})

plot_daily(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name=f"Tmax — {sid}",
    units="°C",
    show=True,
    color="tomato",
    linewidth=0.8,
)

plot_decimals(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    show=True,
)
```

### Variable reference

| Code | Description | Unit (after conversion) |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| `TMAX` | Maximum temperature | °C |
| `TMIN` | Minimum temperature | °C |
| `TAVG` | Average temperature | °C |
| `PRCP` | Precipitation | mm |
| `SNOW` | Snowfall | mm |
| `SNWD` | Snow depth | mm |
| `AWND` | Average wind speed | m/s |

::: {.callout-tip}
Raw GHCN-Daily values for temperature and precipitation are stored in tenths
of the unit. `GHCNDDownloader` divides them by 10 automatically when
`convert_units=True` (the default).
:::

---

## References

- Hunziker, S., et al. (2017). Errors in climate data with emphasis on the
  European region. *Int. J. Climatol.*, 37, 4131–4145.
  <https://doi.org/10.1002/joc.5001>

- Hunziker, S., et al. (2018). Identifying, attributing, and overcoming
  common data quality issues. *Clim. Past*, 14, 1–20.
  <https://doi.org/10.5194/cp-14-1-2018>

- dataresqc R package: <https://github.com/c3s-data-rescue-service/dataresqc>
